title: Closure Trap
description:
The function `make_multipliers(n)` should return a list of `n` functions, where the i-th function multiplies its argument by `i`.

So `make_multipliers(4)[2](5)` should return `10` (2 × 5). But every single function in the list behaves identically — they all multiply by the **same** number.

This is one of Python's most infamous gotchas. The code looks right. But it isn't.
hints: Call `make_multipliers(3)` and then call each function with the same argument, say `10`. What do all three return?
The `lambda x: x * i` captures `i` by **reference**, not by value. When the lambda is finally called, `i` has already reached its last value from the loop.
Fix: capture the current value of `i` at lambda creation time using a default argument: `lambda x, i=i: x * i`. Default arguments are evaluated when the function is defined.
